meta.name: thermoradiobiology_temperature_radiation
meta.description: Machine-readable expert knowledge base for combined hyperthermia and radiotherapy effects on cancer cells and tumors.

evidence.title: Oxygenation of malignant tumors after localized microwave hyperthermia
evidence.summary: Around 40 C oxygenation improved; at higher temperatures oxygenation decreased because of vascular restriction.

evidence.title: Improvement of tumor oxygenation by mild hyperthermia
evidence.summary: Mild hyperthermia can improve tumor oxygenation during and up to 1-2 days after heating and enhance radiation response.

evidence.title: Effects of hyperthermia on DNA repair pathways: one treatment to inhibit them all
evidence.summary: Hyperthermia perturbs multiple repair pathways and proteins, supporting multi-pathway radiosensitization.

evidence.title: Mild hyperthermia inhibits homologous recombination, induces BRCA2 degradation, and sensitizes cancer cells to poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase-1 inhibition
evidence.summary: Mild hyperthermia suppresses homologous recombination and degrades BRCA2.

evidence.title: The effect of thermal dose on hyperthermia-mediated inhibition of DNA repair through homologous recombination
evidence.summary: Thermal dose matters; 41-43 C for about 30-60 min is a key sensitizing window.

evidence.title: Radiosensitization by Hyperthermia: The Effects of Temperature, Sequence, and Time Interval in Cervical Cell Lines
evidence.summary: Short intervals increased residual DNA damage, apoptosis, and cell kill; sequence had similar effects on survival.

evidence.title: Radiosensitization by Hyperthermia Critically Depends on the Time Interval
evidence.summary: Interval critically influenced radiosensitization across preclinical and translational settings.

evidence.title: The Effect of the Time Interval Between Radiation and Hyperthermia on Clinical Outcome in 400 Locally Advanced Cervical Carcinoma Patients
evidence.summary: Interval alone was not dominant once thermal dose and treatment quality were considered.

evidence.title: Randomized trial of hyperthermia and radiation for superficial tumors
evidence.summary: Adequate thermal dose improved complete response; superficial lesions were favored when heating quality was sufficient.

evidence.title: Combined Hyperthermia and Radiation Therapy for Treatment of Hepatocellular Carcinoma
evidence.summary: In HepG2 cells the strongest pattern was 40 C plus 4 Gy plus 48 h with high apoptosis and reduced angiogenic markers.

evidence.title: Clinical Evidence for Thermometric Parameters to Guide Hyperthermia Treatment
evidence.summary: Thermometric parameters and thermal dose are major reproducible predictors.

evidence.title: Hyperthermia and radiotherapy with or without chemotherapy in locally advanced cervical cancer: a systematic review with conventional and network meta-analyses
evidence.summary: HTRT improved complete response and loco-regional control compared with RT.

evidence.title: Phase II clinical trial assessing the addition of hyperthermia to salvage concurrent chemoradiotherapy for unresectable recurrent head and neck cancer in previously irradiated patients
evidence.summary: Repeated HT within 2 h after RT gave high response with manageable toxicity.

rules.note: Mild HT mainly contributes through reoxygenation and perfusion support.
This rule captures the HR inhibition window centered around 41-43 C.
Short HT-RT intervals usually increase residual DNA damage and cell kill.

rules.exception: If thermal dose is adequate or high, interval alone may become a weaker predictor.

rules.note: Sequence is usually less important than interval in cervical cell lines.
High-quality heating and repeated sessions raise response probability.
Depth acts through heating difficulty and reduced achievable thermal dose.
Above 43 C, direct kill rises but vascular support may worsen.

rules.exception: This is a local pilot-study rule and should not be treated as universal.

rules.note: Repeated combined schedules can provide benefit with manageable toxicity.

conflicts.statement_b: Within routine cervical practice, interval alone was not independently dominant when thermal dose was considered.
conflicts.resolution: Model interval as a weighted context-dependent rule and prioritize thermal dose quality during inference.

conflicts.statement_a: Mild HT supports oxygenation and can improve radiation response.
conflicts.statement_b: Higher temperatures increase direct cytotoxicity but may worsen perfusion and toxicity.
conflicts.resolution: Use separate fuzzy regions for mild/sensitizing and cytotoxic-high temperature windows.
